impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of the patent holder's unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the unpatented products' having substantial non-infringing uses defeats a patent-misuse defense to antitrust liability.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act. Ingevity conditioned licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' agreements to exclusively purchase Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld the finding that the honeycombs had substantial non-infringing uses (e.g., air-intake systems outside the patent's scope), defeating Ingevity's argument that its conduct was protected by the patent laws.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors of dominant patent holders benefit from clearer boundaries against leveraging patent rights to foreclose competition in unpatented product markets.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face heightened antitrust risk when bundling patent licenses with purchase obligations for unpatented goods, even where the goods relate to the patented technology, if those goods have substantial non-infringing uses.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls made in an emulator to a model of expected function calls are directed to an abstract idea under Alice step one, and whether damages for foreign sales of infringing software are recoverable.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million infringement judgment, holding that Columbia's asserted cybersecurity patent claims were abstract at Alice step one and remanding for step-two analysis. The court also held that the district court erred in permitting damages based on Norton's foreign sales. The decision impacts software and cybersecurity patents broadly.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against software patent assertions gain a stronger basis to challenge cybersecurity-related patents as abstract under § 101, and can argue against extraterritorial damages for foreign sales.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the software and cybersecurity space face increased vulnerability to § 101 challenges, and universities and other licensors may struggle to recover damages tied to worldwide sales of accused products.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether summary judgment was properly granted on inequitable conduct and Walker Process fraud claims where the patent applicant possessed prior art documents (CYMAX Documents) describing overlapping technology and failed to disclose them during prosecution.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct and Walker Process antitrust fraud, finding genuine disputes of material fact on both issues. Tenaris acquired prior art documents describing a precursor product with overlapping characteristics to its patented coiled tubing and allegedly failed to disclose them to the PTO. The court held that whether this constituted deceptive intent sufficient for inequitable conduct or fraud on the PTO required trial.
impacts.positiveImplications: Companies accused of patent infringement have stronger grounds to pursue inequitable conduct and Walker Process antitrust defenses where a patent applicant acquired and failed to disclose highly material prior art.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders who acquire businesses or assets containing prior art face significant litigation risk if they fail to disclose those materials during patent prosecution, potentially exposing them to both unenforceability and antitrust liability.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to a server tailoring content presentation specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are patent-ineligible abstract ideas under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed a jury verdict for GoTV on Netflix's cross-appeal, holding that patents claiming server-side tailoring of content to wireless device capabilities were directed to ineligible abstract ideas under § 101. The court entered judgment for Netflix, eliminating a $2.5 million damages award. The decision reinforces § 101 limitations on patents claiming conventional content adaptation for different devices.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and technology companies gain a stronger defense against patent assertions covering routine content adaptation for different device form factors.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the media streaming and content delivery space face increased risk that claims involving server-side content tailoring will be deemed abstract and ineligible under § 101.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a respondent in an ITC proceeding may obtain a mandatory stay under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) of a refiled declaratory judgment action after missing the deadline to seek a stay of the original action involving the same parties and issues.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that BioMarin could not obtain a mandatory stay of Ascendis's refiled declaratory judgment action under § 1659(a) after having missed the statutory deadline for seeking a stay in the original action. The court prevented a party from circumventing the statutory time limits by voluntarily dismissing and refiling a parallel district court action to reset the stay clock.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent challengers benefit from certainty that ITC respondents cannot manipulate the mandatory stay provision by engineering a refiling of parallel district court litigation to restart deadlines.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents who miss the 30-day statutory deadline to seek a mandatory stay of parallel district court litigation lose the ability to obtain that stay, even through strategic refiling of the district court action.

impacts.caseName: Apple Inc. v. International Trade Commission
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple's importation and sale of Apple Watch models with blood oxygen measurement features violated Section 337 of the Tariff Act by infringing Masimo's patents on wearable pulse oximetry technology.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated Section 337 by importing Apple Watches incorporating blood oxygen functionality covered by Masimo's patents. The court upheld the limited exclusion order barring importation of infringing Apple Watch models, finding no error in the ITC's domestic industry, validity, or infringement determinations. This high-profile decision directly impacts Apple's smartwatch product line.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in the medical device and wearable technology space are bolstered by the affirmation of ITC enforcement against major technology companies integrating patented health-monitoring features.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies incorporating third-party patented health-monitoring features into consumer electronics face significant import exclusion risk, potentially requiring product redesigns or licensing agreements.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment of no remedy based on the exclusion.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the exclusion of Exafer's damages expert and vacated summary judgment for Microsoft, which had been granted solely because the district court excluded the expert report on damages methodology grounds. The court found the district court applied an improperly rigid standard in evaluating the expert's opinions and that the exclusion cascade—from expert exclusion to no-remedy summary judgment—was an abuse of discretion.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from the court's signal that district courts should not reflexively convert Daubert exclusions of damages experts into summary judgment of no remedy without considering whether the deficiencies are curable.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers face the prospect of retrials and extended litigation when damages expert exclusions are overturned, increasing defense costs and settlement pressure.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Patent Trial and Appeal Board governing discretionary denials of inter partes review institution are 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions guiding the Board's exercise of discretion to decline IPR institution are general policy statements exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b). The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution and that the Director retains ultimate non-institution authority insulated from judicial review.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders benefit from the PTO's ability to maintain flexible institution policies without being forced into formal rulemaking, preserving discretionary authority to manage IPR workloads.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that rely on IPRs to challenge patent validity face continued uncertainty from PTO discretionary denial policies that can be changed without public input or formal rulemaking procedures.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTAB correctly construed the term 'encryption information' in claims directed to streaming partly encrypted media content, and whether claims were obvious in light of prior art under the correct construction.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the PTAB's claim construction of 'encryption information' in DivX's patent on streaming encrypted media, vacated the Board's decision upholding the patent, and remanded for further proceedings. The court found the Board's construction was inconsistent with the claim language and specification, which required encryption information to identify encrypted portions of video frames.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming technology companies benefit from a more rigorous application of claim construction standards in IPR proceedings, particularly for digital content protection patents.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent owners in digital media encryption may face renewed obviousness challenges on remand when claim constructions are corrected to narrow the scope of key terms.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding expert testimony on induced infringement and granting JMOL for the defendant in a medical device patent case involving spinal surgery tools.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of two expert witnesses and the grant of JMOL for DePuy, remanding for a new trial. The court found that the district court improperly excluded experts on induced infringement of surgical derotation tool patents, depriving the patent holder of the ability to present his infringement case. This decision has significant implications for medical device patent enforcement.
impacts.positiveImplications: Individual inventors and small patent holders benefit from reinforced protections against improper exclusion of expert testimony that effectively eliminates their ability to prove infringement at trial.
impacts.negativeImplications: Medical device companies face renewed exposure to induced infringement claims where expert testimony on surgeon use of accused tools is permitted on retrial.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether means-plus-function claim limitations in a patent directed to crop harvester header height control are indefinite when the specification discloses corresponding structure using terms like 'conventional in design and operation.'
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's finding that the asserted claims of a crop harvester patent were indefinite, holding that the specification adequately disclosed corresponding structure for means-plus-function limitations. The court found that the specification's description of controller interface, head controller, and hydraulic control system components was sufficient, even though characterized as 'conventional,' to inform a person of ordinary skill of the claimed structure.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with means-plus-function claims benefit from a more flexible standard for disclosing corresponding structure, particularly when the specification describes well-known components using terms like 'conventional.'
impacts.negativeImplications: Agricultural equipment manufacturers and other accused infringers face renewed infringement proceedings where indefiniteness defenses are rejected on patents with broadly described corresponding structures.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations as confidential is consistent with the governing statute and the common law right of public access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of International Trade's holding that the ITC's blanket confidentiality designation of all questionnaire responses in trade remedy investigations is inconsistent with the statute and does not abrogate the common law right of access to judicial proceedings. The decision requires the ITC to apply the statutory standard for confidential treatment rather than automatically designating all responses as confidential.
impacts.positiveImplications: Domestic industries, trade associations, and the public gain greater transparency in antidumping and countervailing duty proceedings, enabling better-informed participation and oversight of trade enforcement.
impacts.negativeImplications: Foreign producers and importers who submit detailed business information in ITC questionnaire responses face increased risk of disclosure, potentially discouraging candid responses and complicating trade investigations.

impacts.caseName: National Veterans Legal Services Program v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary charged excessive PACER fees to fund expenses unrelated to operating the PACER system.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed approval of a $125 million settlement in a nationwide class action alleging the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund non-PACER expenses. The court upheld the settlement terms, attorneys' fees, and incentive awards to class representatives, rejecting objections that the settlement was inadequate or that the district court lacked jurisdiction under the Little Tucker Act.
impacts.positiveImplications: Businesses and law firms that are heavy PACER users will receive reimbursement for fees charged during the class period, and the settlement establishes accountability for governmental fee-setting practices.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the Restaurant Revitalization Fund statute's mandatory language ('shall award grants') created a money-mandating obligation sufficient to establish Tucker Act jurisdiction for restaurants that applied for but did not receive RRF grants.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the Court of Federal Claims' denial of the government's motion to dismiss, holding that over 300 restaurants stated viable claims for damages under the Tucker Act. The court found that the RRF statute's directive that the SBA 'shall award grants' in the order applications were received created a money-mandating obligation, and that the SBA's failure to follow this order gave rise to actionable claims even after Congress rescinded unobligated RRF funds.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses that were improperly passed over for mandatory government grant programs may pursue damages claims, establishing that statutory 'shall' language creates enforceable money-mandating obligations.
impacts.negativeImplications: The government faces potential liability for hundreds of millions in damages where agencies fail to follow statutory grant-ordering requirements, even after appropriated funds are rescinded.

impacts.caseName: Willis Electric Co., Ltd. v. Polygroup Ltd. (Macao Commercial Offshore)
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether there was substantial evidence to support the jury's finding of non-obviousness of a patent on pre-lit artificial trees with coaxial trunk connectors, and whether the damages award was supported by the evidence.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the denial of Polygroup's JMOL motion on obviousness and damages for Willis's patent on pre-lit artificial tree trunk connectors. The court upheld the jury's non-obviousness finding, concluding that the prior art did not render the claimed coaxial trunk connector configuration obvious. The decision preserves a damages award against a major holiday décor manufacturer.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders in consumer product markets benefit from the court's deference to jury findings on non-obviousness where the prior art does not clearly suggest the claimed combination.
impacts.negativeImplications: Consumer product manufacturers face continued exposure to patent infringement damages where attempts to invalidate patents on obviousness grounds are rejected at trial and upheld on appeal.
